passage: For decades, economists treated the Great Migration — the movement of some six million African Americans from the rural South to northern cities between 1916 and 1970 — primarily as a story of individual ambition. Recent scholarship complicates that picture. Historians now emphasize the dense networks of kinship and church that channeled migrants toward particular neighborhoods and industries, so that a family from a single Mississippi county might reconstitute itself, block by block, in Chicago. On this account, the migration was less a scattering of isolated strivers than a collective transplantation of communities. Yet the older view retains a kernel of truth: the decision to leave, whatever the networks that shaped it, still demanded an act of nerve that many of the migrants' own neighbors were unwilling to make.
stem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: refuting the claim that individual ambition played any role in the Great Migration
tracing how recent scholarship has revised the understanding of the Great Migration
arguing that kinship networks were the sole cause of the Great Migration
comparing the Great Migration with other mass migrations of the period
cataloguing the industries that northern migrants most often entered

solutionStandard: The passage lays out an older view (individual ambition), the newer view (community networks), and then qualifies the new view by granting the old one a "kernel of truth." That arc is a revision of understanding — choice (B). (A) and (C) are too absolute (the passage preserves a role for individual nerve and never calls networks the "sole" cause); (D) offers no comparison; (E) names a detail, not the main concern.

passage: For decades, economists treated the Great Migration — the movement of some six million African Americans from the rural South to northern cities between 1916 and 1970 — primarily as a story of individual ambition. Recent scholarship complicates that picture. Historians now emphasize the dense networks of kinship and church that channeled migrants toward particular neighborhoods and industries, so that a family from a single Mississippi county might reconstitute itself, block by block, in Chicago. On this account, the migration was less a scattering of isolated strivers than a collective transplantation of communities. Yet the older view retains a kernel of truth: the decision to leave, whatever the networks that shaped it, still demanded an act of nerve that many of the migrants' own neighbors were unwilling to make.
stem: The "kernel of truth" that the author grants the older view is that

options.text: migrants acted without any support from their communities
the decision to leave still demanded individual courage
economists had entirely ignored kinship networks
most of the migrants' Southern neighbors also chose to migrate
the migration was driven mainly by economic forces

solutionStandard: The final sentence spells out the kernel of truth: leaving "demanded an act of nerve" — individual courage — choice (B). (A) contradicts the passage's emphasis on networks; (D) contradicts "neighbors were unwilling to make"; (C) describes the new scholarship, not the kernel; (E) is never singled out as the older view's valid core.

passage: For decades, economists treated the Great Migration — the movement of some six million African Americans from the rural South to northern cities between 1916 and 1970 — primarily as a story of individual ambition. Recent scholarship complicates that picture. Historians now emphasize the dense networks of kinship and church that channeled migrants toward particular neighborhoods and industries, so that a family from a single Mississippi county might reconstitute itself, block by block, in Chicago. On this account, the migration was less a scattering of isolated strivers than a collective transplantation of communities. Yet the older view retains a kernel of truth: the decision to leave, whatever the networks that shaped it, still demanded an act of nerve that many of the migrants' own neighbors were unwilling to make.
stem: The example of a family that reconstitutes itself "block by block" in Chicago is offered chiefly to illustrate

options.text: the individual-ambition thesis favored by earlier economists
how kinship and church networks shaped where migrants settled
the economic pull exerted by northern industry
the reluctance of Southern neighbors to leave
the sheer scale of the migration

solutionStandard: The example directly supports the sentence about "dense networks of kinship and church that channeled migrants toward particular neighborhoods" — choice (B). The other options name ideas the passage raises elsewhere but not what this specific example is illustrating.

passage: The popular image of the forest as a battleground — each tree straining upward to steal light from its neighbors — has lately been softened by studies of mycorrhizal networks, the underground webs of fungi that link the roots of different trees. Through these fungal threads, older trees can apparently transfer sugars to shaded seedlings, even seedlings of other species. Some writers have leapt from such findings to a vision of the forest as a harmonious community. Ecologists urge caution. The fungi, after all, are not altruists: they take a substantial cut of the sugar they ferry, and a tree that feeds a rival's seedling may simply be paying an unavoidable toll to a fungal middleman. Cooperation and competition, in this view, may be less opposites than two faces of a single transaction.
stem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: prove that trees do not in fact compete for light
defend the view that forests are harmonious communities
qualify a cooperative interpretation of forest ecology by noting the fungi's self-interest
explain in technical detail how mycorrhizal fungi transfer sugar
reject the recent findings about fungal networks as unreliable

solutionStandard: The passage reports the cooperative findings, notes that "some writers" over-read them, and then, siding with cautious ecologists, complicates the harmony story by pointing to the fungi's cut. That is a qualification — choice (C). (B) is the view being tempered; (A) overstates; (D) is a detail; (E) is wrong because the author accepts the findings, only their rosy interpretation is challenged.

passage: The popular image of the forest as a battleground — each tree straining upward to steal light from its neighbors — has lately been softened by studies of mycorrhizal networks, the underground webs of fungi that link the roots of different trees. Through these fungal threads, older trees can apparently transfer sugars to shaded seedlings, even seedlings of other species. Some writers have leapt from such findings to a vision of the forest as a harmonious community. Ecologists urge caution. The fungi, after all, are not altruists: they take a substantial cut of the sugar they ferry, and a tree that feeds a rival's seedling may simply be paying an unavoidable toll to a fungal middleman. Cooperation and competition, in this view, may be less opposites than two faces of a single transaction.
stem: The statement that the fungi "take a substantial cut of the sugar they ferry" functions to

options.text: prove that the fungi harm the trees they connect
show that the sugar transfer also benefits the fungi, not only the trees
establish that seedlings cannot survive without fungal help
demonstrate that trees prefer to feed only their own species
suggest that ecologists reject the network studies

solutionStandard: The "cut" shows the fungi profit from the exchange, undercutting the picture of pure altruism — choice (B). (A) overstates "harm"; (C) and (D) are not supported; (E) misreads the ecologists, who accept the studies but resist the harmonious reading.

passage: The popular image of the forest as a battleground — each tree straining upward to steal light from its neighbors — has lately been softened by studies of mycorrhizal networks, the underground webs of fungi that link the roots of different trees. Through these fungal threads, older trees can apparently transfer sugars to shaded seedlings, even seedlings of other species. Some writers have leapt from such findings to a vision of the forest as a harmonious community. Ecologists urge caution. The fungi, after all, are not altruists: they take a substantial cut of the sugar they ferry, and a tree that feeds a rival's seedling may simply be paying an unavoidable toll to a fungal middleman. Cooperation and competition, in this view, may be less opposites than two faces of a single transaction.
stem: The author's attitude toward the writers who envision a "harmonious community" is best described as

options.text: wholehearted agreement
complete indifference
measured skepticism
bitter hostility
reverent admiration

solutionStandard: The author reports these writers "leapt" to their vision and then sides with ecologists who "urge caution" — doubt, but not contempt. "Measured skepticism" (C) captures it. (A) and (E) misread the pushback; (B) ignores that the author bothers to correct them; (D) is too strong for the even tone.

passage: The letters reveal a composer very different from the serene genius of legend. In his correspondence, Haydn haggles over fees, complains about drafty lodgings, and frets that a rival will be paid more. Far from diminishing him, these grumblings humanize a figure long frozen into marble. They remind us that the symphonies we revere were produced not in Olympian calm but amid the ordinary anxieties of a working musician who needed his commissions in order to eat.
stem: The author discusses Haydn's complaints about fees and lodgings mainly in order to

options.text: suggest that Haydn was, after all, a mediocre composer
argue that Haydn's anxieties damaged the quality of his music
revise an idealized image of the composer by stressing his everyday concerns
prove that Haydn was consistently underpaid for his work
correct errors of dating in the standard accounts of his life

solutionStandard: The letters show "a composer very different from the serene genius of legend," and the grumblings "humanize a figure long frozen into marble" — the point is to replace an idealized image with a more human one, choice (C). (A) and (B) are contradicted by "far from diminishing him" and by the reverence for the symphonies; (D) and (E) fixate on details the passage does not argue.

passage: The letters reveal a composer very different from the serene genius of legend. In his correspondence, Haydn haggles over fees, complains about drafty lodgings, and frets that a rival will be paid more. Far from diminishing him, these grumblings humanize a figure long frozen into marble. They remind us that the symphonies we revere were produced not in Olympian calm but amid the ordinary anxieties of a working musician who needed his commissions in order to eat.
stem: As used in the passage, the phrase "frozen into marble" most nearly refers to

options.text: an actual memorial statue of the composer
the cold critical reception of Haydn's music
the rigid, idealized reputation that obscured Haydn's humanity
the financial hardship Haydn endured
the technical difficulty of his late symphonies

solutionStandard: "Frozen into marble" is a metaphor for the stiff, monument-like legend of the "serene genius" that the letters humanize — choice (C). (A) reads the metaphor literally; (B), (D), and (E) import ideas the phrase does not carry.

passage: Proponents of universal preschool often cite the Perry Preschool study, whose participants, decades later, earned more and were arrested less than a control group. Critics raise two objections. First, the sample was tiny — barely a hundred children — so its striking results may not generalize. Second, the program was unusually intensive and expensive, staffed by highly trained teachers; a nationwide program run on a fraction of the budget might deliver only a fraction of the benefit. Neither objection shows that preschool fails to work, but together they caution against treating one small, gold-plated experiment as a blueprint for national policy.
stem: According to the passage, critics of relying on the Perry study raise which of the following objections? Select all that apply.

options.text: The study's sample was too small for its results to generalize confidently.
The program was more intensive and costly than a nationwide program would be.
The study found no lasting benefit from preschool attendance.

solutionStandard: The passage names exactly two objections: the "tiny" sample (A) and the program's unusual intensity and expense (B). (C) is false — the study found participants "earned more and were arrested less," and even the critics concede the objections don't show "preschool fails to work."

passage: Proponents of universal preschool often cite the Perry Preschool study, whose participants, decades later, earned more and were arrested less than a control group. Critics raise two objections. First, the sample was tiny — barely a hundred children — so its striking results may not generalize. Second, the program was unusually intensive and expensive, staffed by highly trained teachers; a nationwide program run on a fraction of the budget might deliver only a fraction of the benefit. Neither objection shows that preschool fails to work, but together they caution against treating one small, gold-plated experiment as a blueprint for national policy.
stem: The author's own position on the Perry study is best described as

options.text: enthusiastic endorsement of it as a model for national policy
dismissal of it as worthless evidence
cautious respect that stops short of treating it as a blueprint
hostility toward the proponents of universal preschool
indifference to what the study found

solutionStandard: The author grants that "neither objection shows that preschool fails to work" yet warns against "treating one small, gold-plated experiment as a blueprint" — respect tempered by caution, choice (C). (A) is the very move the author resists; (B) and (E) ignore the acknowledged benefits; (D) mischaracterizes the balanced tone.

passage: Economists have long puzzled over why people tip in restaurants they will never visit again. Standard theory predicts that a rational diner, facing no threat of worse service in the future, would leave nothing. Yet tipping persists even among tourists in distant cities. One explanation appeals to internalized social norms: the discomfort of violating a rule one has absorbed since childhood outweighs the few dollars saved. A competing account is simpler — most diners never perform the cold calculation the theory imagines, and instead just do what those around them do. The two explanations are difficult to separate, since both predict the same generous behavior.
stem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: proving that the practice of tipping is fundamentally irrational
arguing that internalized social norms fully explain why people tip
considering why people tip even when self-interest predicts they would not
recommending that diners stop tipping in restaurants they will not revisit
comparing tipping customs in different countries

solutionStandard: The passage sets up a puzzle — tipping persists where standard theory says it shouldn't — and weighs two explanations for it. That is choice (C). (B) picks one explanation the passage treats as merely one of two; (A), (D), and (E) describe things the passage never does.

passage: Economists have long puzzled over why people tip in restaurants they will never visit again. Standard theory predicts that a rational diner, facing no threat of worse service in the future, would leave nothing. Yet tipping persists even among tourists in distant cities. One explanation appeals to internalized social norms: the discomfort of violating a rule one has absorbed since childhood outweighs the few dollars saved. A competing account is simpler — most diners never perform the cold calculation the theory imagines, and instead just do what those around them do. The two explanations are difficult to separate, since both predict the same generous behavior.
stem: According to the passage, "standard theory" predicts that a diner at a restaurant never to be revisited would

options.text: tip generously out of ingrained habit
leave no tip at all
tip strictly in proportion to the quality of service
imitate the behavior of nearby diners
leave only a very small tip

solutionStandard: The passage states that standard theory predicts "a rational diner ... would leave nothing" — choice (B). The other options describe the actual behavior the theory fails to predict, or the rival explanations, not the theory's own prediction.

passage: Economists have long puzzled over why people tip in restaurants they will never visit again. Standard theory predicts that a rational diner, facing no threat of worse service in the future, would leave nothing. Yet tipping persists even among tourists in distant cities. One explanation appeals to internalized social norms: the discomfort of violating a rule one has absorbed since childhood outweighs the few dollars saved. A competing account is simpler — most diners never perform the cold calculation the theory imagines, and instead just do what those around them do. The two explanations are difficult to separate, since both predict the same generous behavior.
stem: The author states that the two explanations are "difficult to separate" mainly because

options.text: both explanations have been shown to be false
they lead to the same prediction about how diners behave
neither explanation has any supporting evidence
economists have refused to test either one
tipping varies too much from person to person

solutionStandard: The final sentence gives the reason directly: the explanations are hard to separate "since both predict the same generous behavior" — choice (B). If two accounts predict identical behavior, observing that behavior can't tell them apart. The other options are not stated.

passage: The conventional account credits the Industrial Revolution to a handful of ingenious inventors — Watt, Arkwright, Stephenson — whose machines transformed the world. Recent economic history tells a less heroic story. The crucial inventions, these historians argue, were rarely the work of a single mind; they emerged from dense networks of tinkerers who shared, copied, and incrementally improved one another's designs. Watt's engine, for example, depended on precision cylinders that only an existing community of instrument-makers could supply. The lone genius, on this view, is less an explanation than a convenient fiction — a way of assigning to one person credit that in truth belonged to many.
stem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: proving that Watt did not build his own steam engine
defending the traditional view that a few inventors caused the Industrial Revolution
revising the "lone genius" account by emphasizing collaborative networks of tinkerers
explaining in detail how the steam engine works
ranking the most important inventions of the industrial era

solutionStandard: The passage contrasts the "conventional account" of heroic inventors with recent history that credits collaborative networks, calling the lone genius a "convenient fiction." That is a revision of the account — choice (C). (B) is the view being challenged; (A) overstates; (D) and (E) are not the focus.

passage: The conventional account credits the Industrial Revolution to a handful of ingenious inventors — Watt, Arkwright, Stephenson — whose machines transformed the world. Recent economic history tells a less heroic story. The crucial inventions, these historians argue, were rarely the work of a single mind; they emerged from dense networks of tinkerers who shared, copied, and incrementally improved one another's designs. Watt's engine, for example, depended on precision cylinders that only an existing community of instrument-makers could supply. The lone genius, on this view, is less an explanation than a convenient fiction — a way of assigning to one person credit that in truth belonged to many.
stem: The author mentions the precision cylinders that Watt's engine required chiefly in order to

options.text: show that Watt worked entirely on his own
illustrate how even a celebrated invention depended on a wider community of makers
argue that precision cylinders were the era's single most important invention
suggest that instrument-makers deserve no credit for the engine
prove that early steam engines were impractical

solutionStandard: The cylinders example supports the claim that inventions "emerged from dense networks": even Watt's famous engine relied on other makers — choice (B). (A) and (D) are the opposite of the point; (C) and (E) are not claimed.

passage: The conventional account credits the Industrial Revolution to a handful of ingenious inventors — Watt, Arkwright, Stephenson — whose machines transformed the world. Recent economic history tells a less heroic story. The crucial inventions, these historians argue, were rarely the work of a single mind; they emerged from dense networks of tinkerers who shared, copied, and incrementally improved one another's designs. Watt's engine, for example, depended on precision cylinders that only an existing community of instrument-makers could supply. The lone genius, on this view, is less an explanation than a convenient fiction — a way of assigning to one person credit that in truth belonged to many.
stem: The phrase "convenient fiction" suggests that the author regards the lone-genius story as

options.text: an accurate if incomplete account of events
a deliberate lie told by the inventors themselves
a harmless myth with no real consequences
a misleading simplification that credits one person for the work of many
the best explanation currently available

solutionStandard: "Convenient fiction" and "assigning to one person credit that belonged to many" mark the story as a tidy but false simplification — choice (D). (A) and (E) treat it as basically correct; (B) overstates it as an intentional lie; (C) ignores that the author thinks it misassigns credit.

passage: Biologists once assumed that birdsong served a single purpose: males advertising for mates. Field studies have complicated this tidy picture. In many species females sing too, sometimes in duets that appear to coordinate defense of a shared territory rather than to attract partners. Song, it turns out, can carry different messages in different contexts — a claim to territory, a warning, a bond between mates. The old single-function model was not so much wrong as incomplete, mistaking one use of a versatile tool for its only use.
stem: The passage mainly argues that birdsong

options.text: is produced only by female birds
serves several different functions rather than one
serves only to attract mates
was entirely misunderstood by earlier biologists
consists mainly of coordinated duets

solutionStandard: The passage shows song can be "a claim to territory, a warning, a bond between mates" — multiple functions, choice (B). (C) is the old view being complicated; (A) and (E) overstate the female-duet detail; (D) is too strong (the model was "incomplete," not entirely wrong).

passage: Biologists once assumed that birdsong served a single purpose: males advertising for mates. Field studies have complicated this tidy picture. In many species females sing too, sometimes in duets that appear to coordinate defense of a shared territory rather than to attract partners. Song, it turns out, can carry different messages in different contexts — a claim to territory, a warning, a bond between mates. The old single-function model was not so much wrong as incomplete, mistaking one use of a versatile tool for its only use.
stem: The female duets are cited as evidence that song can

options.text: attract mates more effectively than male song
coordinate the defense of a territory
warn other species of predators
replace male song entirely
confirm the single-function model

solutionStandard: The passage says the duets "appear to coordinate defense of a shared territory rather than to attract partners" — choice (B). (A) and (D) overstate; (C) is a different function not tied to the duets; (E) is the opposite of the point.

passage: Biologists once assumed that birdsong served a single purpose: males advertising for mates. Field studies have complicated this tidy picture. In many species females sing too, sometimes in duets that appear to coordinate defense of a shared territory rather than to attract partners. Song, it turns out, can carry different messages in different contexts — a claim to territory, a warning, a bond between mates. The old single-function model was not so much wrong as incomplete, mistaking one use of a versatile tool for its only use.
stem: The author regards the old single-function model as

options.text: completely false
deliberately deceptive
the most accurate model still available
incomplete but not wholly wrong
irrelevant to modern biology

solutionStandard: The final sentence says the model was "not so much wrong as incomplete" — it captured one real function but missed the others, choice (D). (A) and (B) overstate; (C) and (E) misread the author, who treats it as superseded but partly right.
